steps.title: Identify the independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses: “They are hardly [word]” and “many chondrites experience aqueous alteration as a result of exposure to fluids, as well as fracturing, veining, and localized melting due to collisions with other objects.” The blank needs “pristine”—they are hardly pristine means they are not in good condition. These two complete thoughts must be properly separated by punctuation.

steps.title: Apply the boundary rule for joining independent clauses
steps.content:
A semicolon separates two independent clauses; a comma alone creates a comma splice. “Though” serves as a transitional word showing the relationship between the clauses. Option A omits the required semicolon, leaving only a comma between the clauses. Option C places the semicolon incorrectly—before “though” instead of between the main clauses. Option D uses commas around “though” as a parenthetical, which doesn't properly separate the independent clauses. Option B correctly inserts a semicolon to mark the boundary between the two independent clauses: “They are hardly pristine, though; many chondrites experience...”

That matches choice B — B is correct.
